yokohama.alt: People walking down a narrow street with shops and signs.

Tokyo streets are all about myriads of tiny details. You always spot something new, even if you walk the same street every day. This picture was taken near Asakusa Park with iPhone 5S, no edits were applied. I was amazed with the quality of the image. Crisp details, amazing colors. HDR made a miracle with this one.
yokohama.attribution: Photo by Roman Davydko on Unsplash

akihabara.alt: 秋葉原中央通交差点から昌平橋方向を向いた写真。ゲームセンターや書店、電子パーツ店など様々な店が軒を連ねている。 Photo looking toward Shoheibashi from the Akihabara Chuodori intersection. Various stores such as game centers, bookstores, and electronic parts stores line the street.
akihabara.attribution: Photo by taro ohtani on Unsplash
Photo by Svetlana Gumerova on Unsplash

akihabara.alt:
The hill leading up to the main entrance of the Kanda Shrine.

Kanda Shrine (神田明神 Kanda-myōjin, officially 神田神社 Kanda-jinja), is a Shinto shrine located in Chiyoda, Tokyo, Japan. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas in Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the warrior class and citizens of Japan, especially during the Edo period, when shōgun Tokugawa Ieyasu paid his respects at Kanda Shrine. Due in part to the proximity of the Kanda Shrine to Akihabara, the shrine has become a mecca for the technophiles who frequent Akihabara.
akihabara.attribution: Photo by Xtra, Inc. on Unsplash

kokubunji.alt: A bustling street scene in Kyoto, Japan, captures the vibrant energy of the city under an overcast sky. A prominent Japanese flag waves proudly, overlooking traditional tiled rooftops and a lively crowd of people exploring the charming shops and historic architecture. Distant mountains frame the urban landscape, adding to the picturesque view

tsurumi.alt: The weather at Fuji can be quite capricious: you can consider yourself lucky if you happen to get a clear view. Some people went there 3 times, only to see Fuji hiding in the clouds. I tried to make the most of this luck and capture this scenery with a personal touch.

higashi-shinjuku.alt: In the Shirahige-Higashi Housing Complex, the devastating 1923 earthquake left many dead due to the surrounding land being a sandbar, leaving no escape routes. To address this, the entire complex was constructed in 1972, designed to act as a 1.1 km-long firewall to offer refuge for its inhabitants. Every building gap is equipped with fire doors and shutters, with the residential balconies similarly fitted with fire shutters. Throughout the area, water cannons have been set up to counteract fire outbreaks, and atop the complex, water storage tanks have been placed for firefighting purposes.
higashi-shinjuku.attribution: Photo by taro ohtani on Unsplash
